title: Chamomilla recutita (German Chamomile)
medicine: German chamomile (Chamomilla recutita) has many health benefits, such as reducing inflammation, aiding digestion, and promoting relaxation. It is commonly used to treat conditions such as skin irritations, insomnia, anxiety, and menstrual cramps.

The medicinal properties of German chamomile are due to its constituents, including flavonoids, polysaccharides, and essential oils. Flavonoids have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, while polysaccharides have antimicrobial and immunomodulatory properties. The most common essential oil in German chamomile is apigenin, which has sedative and anxiolytic effects.

The most commonly used parts of German chamomile for medicinal purposes are the flowers, tea, and essential oil. Flowers can be consumed as a tea or added to bathwater for their calming properties. Essential oil can be used topically for its anti-inflammatory and antiseptic effects or added to a diffuser for its relaxing properties.

While German chamomile is generally considered safe, it may cause side effects in some individuals. These include allergic reactions, such as skin rash, hives, and difficulty breathing. It may also interact with certain medications, including sedatives and anti-anxiety drugs. Pregnant women should avoid consuming German chamomile tea or essential oil, as it may cause contractions.

To ensure the safety and efficacy of German chamomile for medicinal purposes, it is important to use caution. Do not consume large amounts of German chamomile tea or essential oil, as this may lead to toxicity. Consult a healthcare professional before using German chamomile if you have any underlying health conditions or are taking medication. Additionally, do not use German chamomile as a substitute for medical treatment without consulting a healthcare professional.
horticulture: German Chamomile (Chamomilla recutita) is an herb that prefers full sun to partial shade and well-draining soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.5. It grows to a height of 30 to 60 cm and has feathery flowers that are white or yellow in color. The plant can live for up to three years.

To grow German Chamomile, start by preparing the soil with compost or well-rotted manure. Sow seeds about 1 cm deep and 30 cm apart in rows that are spaced about 60 cm apart. Cover the seeds with a thin layer of soil and keep the area moist until germination occurs, which usually takes 7 to 10 days. Thin seedlings to about 15 cm apart when they are about 5 cm tall.

Caring for German Chamomile is relatively easy. Water the plant regularly, especially during dry spells, and mulch with organic matter to retain moisture. Fertilize with an all-purpose fertilizer or compost every 4 to 6 weeks. Deadhead flowers to encourage more blooms and prevent self-pollination.

German Chamomile is ready to harvest when the flower heads are fully opened, which usually occurs in about 60 to 90 days after planting. Cut the flower heads off the stems using scissors or a sharp knife, leaving about an inch of stem above the soil. Dry the flowers in a single layer on a paper towel or in a dehydrator at low heat (125°F/50°C) for 4 to 7 days, turning them occasionally.

German Chamomile is generally pest-free and disease-resistant, but it can be affected by aphids and whiteflies. If you notice any signs of infestation, use insecticidal soap or neem oil to control the pests. German Chamomile is also susceptible to fungal diseases such as powdery mildew and rust, which can be prevented by good air circulation and proper watering techniques.
botany: German Chamomile, with botanical name Chamomilla recutita, belongs to the domain Eukarya, kingdom Plantae, phylum Magnoliophyta, class Liliopsida, order Asterales, family Asteraceae, genus Chamomila, species recutita. The common names include German chamomile, sweet chamomile, and Roman chamomile.

German Chamomile is a perennial herbaceous plant that grows to a height of 30-60 cm. The leaves are linear, grayish-green, and hairy on both sides. The flowers are white, daisy-like, and have a diameter of 1.5-2 cm. They are borne on peduncles that are longer than the leaves. The fruits are small, dark brown, and have a spiny surface.

There are several variants of German Chamomile, including Chamomila recutita var. latifolia, which has wider leaves, and Chamomila recutita var. matricaria, which is smaller and more compact. Chamomila recutita var. latifolia is often called "wild chamomile" because it grows in fields and meadows.
German Chamomile is native to Europe, Asia, and North Africa. It is commonly found in fields, gardens, and along roadsides. It prefers well-drained soil and full sun or partial shade.

The life cycle of German Chamomile is biennial. In the first year, it produces a rosette of leaves that grows to a height of 30-45 cm. The flowers bloom in the second year and develop from a single flower bud at the top of the plant. After the flowers bloom, the plant dies back to the ground and produces new growth in the following year.
